Summary:
Structure: causal chain (role → mechanism → outcome).

In NSCLC, PD-L1 functions as a tumor-expressed ligand of the PD-1 immune checkpoint. Mechanistically, PD-L1 on tumor (and some immune) cells binds PD-1 on recently activated T cells, inhibiting T cell–associated kinases, blocking cytotoxic T lymphocyte responses, reducing T and B cell proliferation and cytokine production, and thereby causing T cell exhaustion. As an outcome, elevated PD-L1 expression in many NSCLC tumors promotes immune escape and is associated with shorter survival and poorer prognosis; high PD-L1 often co-occurs with dense T cell infiltration that reflects T cell dysfunction. Clinically, PD-L1 expression is the preferred biomarker for predicting benefit from anti–PD-1/PD-L1 therapy, though heterogeneity, temporal change, and assay variability limit its predictive consistency.
References: Tang, S., Qin, C., Hu, H., Liu, T., He, Y., Guo, H., Yan, H., Zhang, J., Tang, S., & Zhou, H. (2022). Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: Progress, Challenges, and Prospects. Cells, 11(3), 320. https://doi.org/10.3390/cells11030320
